analogy: Think of "Reproduction in Plants" through the chapter's own opening images: SCIENCE 142 Forests: Our Lifeline 12 O ne evening Boojho entered the park with an elderly person. Prof Ahmad was a scientist working in the university. The children started playing while Prof Ahmad sat on a bench in the corner. He was tired as he... The easy idea is that the visible surface is only the outside cover. The chapter keeps asking how we can know what is inside when no one can directly reach the deep interior. So the learner must read clues: rocks, mines, drilling, volcanic material, heat, gravity, magnetism and earthquake waves. Like solving a hidden-layer puzzle, each clue tells something about the crust, mantle, core and volcanic activity without inventing anything beyond the textbook.
full_notes:
Reproduction in Plants is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins by asking how the nature of the earth should be imagined and why the interior cannot be known in the same direct way as the surface. It connects the hidden interior with visible results on the surface, especially landscape development, earthquakes, tsunamis and volcanic activity.

The text explains that sCIENCE 142 Forests: Our Lifeline 12 O ne evening Boojho entered the park with an elderly person.

The text explains that prof Ahmad was a scientist working in the university.

The text explains that the children started playing while Prof Ahmad sat on a bench in the corner.

The text explains that he was tired as he had participated in the golden jubilee celebrations of the town.

The text explains that after a while, the children also came and sat around him.

The text explains that prof Ahmad told them that after the cultural programme, the senior people discussed the town’s unemployment problem.

The text explains that a plan was proposed to put up a factory by clearing an area of the forest just outside the town.

The text explains that this would give the increasing population of the town a chance to get jobs.

The text explains that the children were very surprised when Prof Ahmad told them that many people had objected to this idea. “This is because the forests serve as green lungs and water purifying systems in nature”, Prof Ahmad explained.

The text explains that prof Ahmad realised that the children had not visited a forest.

The text explains that the children also wanted to know more about the forest, so they decided to visit it with Prof Ahmad.

The text explains that 12.1 V ISIT TO A F OREST One Sunday morning, the children packed a few things like a knife, a hand lens, a stick, a notebook and walked together through a forest trail near a village.

The text explains that on their way, they met Tibu, a young boy of their age group, of nearby village, who was taking cattle for grazing along with his aunt.

The text explains that he was very agile, running here and there to keep the herd together.

The text explains that when he saw the children, Tibu also started walking along with them, while his aunt went on a different path.

The text explains that as soon as they entered the forest Tibu raised his hand and signalled them to keep quiet because noise could disturb the animals living in the forest.

The text explains that however, surprisingly, they saw that the raindrops were not hitting the forest floor directly.

The text explains that on the other hand, if trees are not present, rain hits the ground directly and may flood the area around it.

The text explains that construction of roads, buildings, industrial development and increasing demand of wood created pressure on the forests and it started vanishing.

The text explains that they are a source of medicinal plants, timber and many other useful products.

The text explains that sCIENCE 152 1 If forests disappear, the amount of carbon dioxide in air will increase, resulting in the increase of earth’s temperature.

The text explains that 12.15, the artist has forgotten to put the labels and directions on the arrows.

The text explains that mark the directions on the arrows and label the diagram using the following labels: clouds, rain, atmosphere, carbon dioxide, oxygen, plants, animals, soil, roots, water table.

The text explains that you may not be able to name all the plants, but it is worth recording and seeing where they grow.

The text explains that make a record of approximate heights of plants, crown shape, bark texture, leaf size, and flower colour.

The text explains that (g) Learn to recognise the animal’s droppings.

The text explains that (h) Interview the forest officials and the people of surrounding vil lages and other visitors.

The text explains that you must never collect birds’ eggs, and their nests should never be disturbed.

The text explains that you can read more on the following website: www.wild-india.com Did you know?

The text explains that in India the area under forest cover is about 21% of the total area.

The text explains that it had steadily been falling since independence.

The text explains that but people now seem to have realised the importance of the forest cover.

The text explains that reports suggest that the area under forest cover has slightly increased in recent years.

Source: NCERT Class 7 Science, Chapter 12: Reproduction in Plants

prelims_traps: Do not treat SCIENCE 142 and 12.1 V ISIT TO A F OREST as identical unless the source text links them directly.
Do not add outside examples to Reproduction in Plants; use only the examples and processes named in the chapter.
Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.
mains_angles: Explain Reproduction in Plants through the source chain: evidence, process and visible result.
Use SCIENCE 142 and 12.1 V ISIT TO A F OREST to show how the chapter connects facts with mechanisms.
Frame an answer around causes, internal working, surface expression and source limitations from the NCERT chapter.
